posts.cooked:
San Francisco Chronicle – 21 Apr 16

The cult of Cabernet: The paradox of Napa’s most expensive wines

Perched on a divan in the “tasting room” — it feels like the fanciest living room...

“Today, the mythic quality of these cult estates is running up against a wine culture that may no longer buy the hype. The castles on the hill remain. But has the crowd wandered?”

link_counts.title: The cult of Cabernet: The paradox of Napa’s most expensive wines

posts.cooked:
Thanks for the link, Brett. A rather balanced & well-written article I thought by Esther, whose
writings I’m starting to like a lot. It’s pretty easy to take pot-shots at these high-end Cabernets
and their owners, but Esther pretty much avoided that. I don’t begrudge all the investment bankers
of the World the opportunity to drink these kinds of wines…if they do actually drink them.
I do know that sitting in that Harlan
tasting room would give me the heebie-jeebies. I’d much rather be casually sitting out on a deck up on
Mt.Veeder w/ real folks, knocking back glasses of Tribidrag & Mondeuse, tossing the pits from the home-cured
Estate olives over your shoulder onto the rows of Mondeuse. Now that’s living!!
Tom

posts.name: Arv R@()
posts.cooked: nice pictures too, thanks for sharing
Thanks, enjoyed the article.

posts.name: Markus S
posts.cooked: [video]YouTube

posts.name: Kyle Schlachter
posts.cooked: I like the part about the cult being the consumers (buyers who may not actually consume) the true cult wineries really only raised prices to absurd heights in response to the secondary market. There have been a whole lot of Johnny-come-lately producers that have jumped directly into the pool of absurdity in the hopes of getting into the cult…

posts.name: David Cooper
posts.cooked:
First thing I find flawed about the article is no mention of Gil Nickel and Far Niente. He was the one who started the absurd practice of releasing the first wine at twice as much as the standard bearers. Also he had the douchy packaging that the Napa Auction crowd loved.

Also they seem to have slipped a photo of a Restoration Hardware showroom into the piece.

posts.name: Anton Dotson
posts.cooked:
David Cooper:

First thing I find flawed about the article is no mention of Gil Nickel and Far Niente. He was the one who started the absurd practice of releasing the first wine at twice as much as the standard bearers. Also he had the douchy packaging that the Napa Auction crowd loved.

Also they seem to have slipped a photo of a Restoration Hardware showroom into the piece.

Excellent post.

Far Niente strikes me as my initial “it costs how much?” experience.

Sure is a beautiful place, though. Iconic.

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:
This article reminded me of stories you used to read in the Satyurday Evening Post in the 60s about the Jet Set…although they appear to be having a good time skiing in Gstaad they are really sad and lonely.
Later we all admitted we were jealous.

Yeah she could have left that lonely part out.

I’ve tasted some of the wines over the years, not all, and they’re quite good. I’m not jealous and don’t begrudge anyone the right to enjoy the wines at any price. If they were cheaper, I might even buy some but I don’t feel particularly deprived by not having them. There’s a lot of great wine around these days.

All in all though, it wasn’t a bad piece. Thanks for the link.

I don’t know her - did she replace Bonne?

posts.name: robert creth
posts.cooked: Like many on this board, I have no experience with these wines other that reading about them. Love well made Cabs and figure that the high end players push the level of everyone. I am not a hater and appreciate that they are apparently, well made and good wines. But the question of when do they stop being “wine” resonates. Not a “baller” and I don’t foresee the day that I pay a thousand dollars for any bottle or even half that. I wouldn’t know how to enjoy a wine at that level. I get anxious with bottles over $75. But the excess of “new money” is screwing with everything of any value in California. San Francisco is unrecognizable as the place where you could once ( and not that long ago) move to, find a cheap 1 bedroom and establish a foothold to a career. I worry about being priced out of the wine market also.

posts.name: Vince T s 3 n g
posts.cooked:
Great article.

Re: living in San Francisco… you could trade your 1br in San Francisco for a mortgage on a nice rowhouse in Philly and have enough cash leftover for a bottle of screagle or harlan every month.

posts.name: robert creth
posts.cooked:
Vince T:

Great article.

Re: living in San Francisco… you could trade your 1br in San Francisco for a mortgage on a nice rowhouse in Philly and have enough cash leftover for a bottle of screagle or harlan every month.

True, but then I would be living in Philly, and no amount of Screagle would change that.

posts.name: David_S
posts.cooked: If only all tasting notes were written like that…

posts.name: mark rudner
posts.cooked:
great article, thanks
screaming eagle started in 92 though

posts.name: Drew Goin
posts.cooked: Thanks for sharing. I am not sure that the author casts any new light on the subject, but I do appreciate that she writes more about the wine and its birthplace than many previous “Cult Cab” articles.

posts.name: D Borczon
posts.cooked:
And Screagle is definitely a valley floor wine, not grown on hillsides. It seems the author needs to do a little more due diligence in her writing. Is this really news? I suppose to the average consumer. Wine community consumers and insiders have known about this phenomenon for some time. I think the cult wine tide has about reached the high water mark. I really do hope that the investment bankers move on to a new toy soon like gold or diamonds, let the rest of us winos have our juice back.

In the meantime i suppose it has just driven a lot of people to explore the far reaches of Savoie, Jura, Loire, Central Otago and other more obscure regions to stimulate our tastebuds. Maybe not such a bad thing. However the upcoming burial of Burgundy may be a tragic casualty. I am planning a trip to ride the great Burg vineyards this summer before the real Burgundy can be consumed in a tidal wave of Chinese money and obsessive collectors…

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I thought it was a nicely done piece all things considered. Not as good as her recent Santa Cruz Mountains piece, but still solid.

One might object, as has been mentioned, that Screaming Eagle is not really a hillside property. Neither, for that matter, is Araujo’s Eisele vineyard. Hard not to notice that when one visits.

I also wonder how accurately the heady atmosphere of the period in which the ‘cult’ cabs emerged has been rendered. I was not there to experience it, but one picks things up. According to the accounts I’ve heard, time was when, say, Spottswoode, Dunn and Togni were almost part of the cult cab conversation. When you put a bottle down on the table heads turned. Then there were what one might call the ‘busted flushes’, like Behrens and Hitchcock, Sloan etc than never really made good the early hype, but looked as if they might at one point.

So to make out that ‘cult’ cabs only ever meant Harlan, Screaming Eagle and a handful of others is teleological, limiting what could be a fascinating essay in anthropology and cultural history.

posts.name: Don Maloney
posts.cooked: Thanks for the article. She was too kind though.

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked:
robert creth:

But the excess of “new money” is screwing with everything of any value in California. San Francisco is unrecognizable as the place where you could once ( and not that long ago) move to, find a cheap 1 bedroom and establish a foothold to a career. I worry about being priced out of the wine market also.

There are as expensive and more expensive wines in other parts of the world that have little “new money” around them, too. How much do Dujac, Rousseau, DRC, Salon, Grange, Monfortino, Vega Sicilia and so forth cost?

Certain items at the tops of their categories, and layered on with the prestige factor bonus, are going to sell at prices that limit them to a small few. I’ll never own a Ferrari, I’ll never wear a Patek Phillipe watch, I’ll never own a luxury yacht.

That might be slightly disappointing, but the good news is that I can buy a great car for $30,000 these days, and I can drink excellent wines that cost $25, $50 and $75 a bottle.

I liked the tone of the article, and I appreciate she actually tasted the wines and didn’t do so with some prejudged dislike of them. I’ve had Harlan a few times, and it can be spectacularly good in some vintages. The lament about how few people will get to taste them rang a little hollow - again, you can say the same thing about any expensive product.
